rationale_for_baseline_tier: A new non-blanching palpable purpuric rash mandates same-day in-person assessment to exclude meningococcaemia and severe thrombocytopenia and to perform the urinalysis, blood-pressure check and full blood count that a telehealth service cannot. The patient is currently well, afebrile and haemodynamically stable, so this is NOT an immediate life threat requiring an ambulance (T4/T5); but a new non-blanching rash is far too dangerous a presentation to defer to routine or even prompt GP review (T1/T2). The honest telehealth-appropriate tier is therefore T3 — urgent same-day in-person / urgent-care assessment — with explicit escalation criteria.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fever, rigors, feeling very unwell, or the rash rapidly spreading up onto the trunk/whole body
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with a spreading non-blanching rash is meningococcal sepsis until proven otherwise — a time-critical emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever or the shakes, start to feel really unwell, or the rash spreads quickly onto your body, call 000 for an ambulance straight away and say you have a spreading rash and a fever.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe cramping abdominal pain or vomiting
escalation_edges.rationale: GI involvement of IgA vasculitis can cause bowel wall oedema, bleeding and (rarely) intussusception needing surgical assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get severe, cramping tummy pain or start vomiting, go to your nearest emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Urine turns cola/tea-coloured or bloody, reduced urine output, or new ankle/facial swelling
escalation_edges.rationale: Signals IgA-mediated glomerulonephritis, the complication that drives long-term prognosis, especially in adults.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your urine turns pink, red or cola-coloured, you're passing much less than usual, or your ankles or face swell up, get seen the same day and tell them your kidneys may be involved.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New bleeding from gums or nose, or spontaneous bruising elsewhere on the body
escalation_edges.rationale: Suggests a bleeding diathesis / severe thrombocytopenia needing an urgent full blood count and assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you start bleeding from your gums or nose, or get unexplained bruises elsewhere, go to an emergency department for an urgent blood test.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Joints becoming too painful to walk, or the rash worsening substantially
escalation_edges.rationale: Significant joint involvement or worsening indicates more than skin-limited disease and warrants urgent reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your joints become too painful to walk on, or the rash gets much worse, arrange to be seen again urgently the same day.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Adult IgA vasculitis carries a higher risk of significant renal involvement than the childhood form, so renal surveillance (repeat urinalysis and blood pressure over the following weeks) must be emphasised even though the initial picture is mild.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: You need to be seen in person today — go to your GP or an urgent care clinic so they can examine you, do a urine test, check your blood pressure and take a blood test. These check for the more serious causes of a non-fading rash and make sure your kidneys are not involved; they can't be done safely over a video/chat call. In the meantime rest, keep your legs elevated, and watch for the warning signs.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Same-day in-person GP or urgent care appointment today
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If no same-day GP or urgent care is available, attend an after-hours clinic or emergency department for assessment today; call 000 immediately if you develop a fever with a spreading rash or feel very unwell.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth encounter: new non-blanching palpable purpura confined to both lower legs, ~12 hours, two weeks post-URTI, afebrile and systemically well, no GI/renal/joint symptoms and no mucosal bleeding by history. Requesting in-person exam, urine dipstick, blood pressure and full blood count to exclude meningococcaemia/thrombocytopenia/nephritis; arrange ongoing renal surveillance.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing a new non-blanching purpuric rash as benign and advising self-care or routine/prompt GP review without urgent same-day in-person assessment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A missed meningococcaemia, severe thrombocytopenia, or occult IgA nephritis progresses because the dangerous causes were never assessed with an examination, urinalysis and full blood count.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recognising some concern but deferring to a 24-48 hour GP appointment rather than same-day assessment for an unassessed non-blanching rash, risking delayed detection of renal involvement or a bleeding disorder.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Activating a 000 ambulance for a well, afebrile, haemodynamically stable adult driven by the patient's fear of infection — unnecessary emergency-ambulance use and resource diversion when calibrated same-day in-person assessment is appropriate.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot exclude meningococcaemia, thrombocytopenia or occult nephritis, and cannot confirm the benign diagnosis, because the deciding tests (examination, urinalysis, blood pressure, full blood count) are all unavailable remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the most likely benign cause honestly while making clear it can only be confirmed in person today; arrange urgent same-day assessment with urinalysis/BP/FBC and give explicit red-flag safety-netting.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either providing definitive remote reassurance that it is a harmless rash, or over-reacting to the patient's fear by dispatching an ambulance for a well, stable patient.
